title: EXPOSÉ XVIII WEIL'S THEOREM ON THE CONSTRUCTION OF A GROUP FROM A RATIONAL LAW

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XVIII WEIL'S THEOREM ON THE CONSTRUCTION OF A GROUP FROM A RATIONAL LAW

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XVIII
WEIL'S THEOREM ON THE CONSTRUCTION OF A GROUP FROM A RATIONAL LAW0by Michael Artin

footnotes.html: (0) version of May 8, 09

blocks.html: 0. Introduction

This exposé is devoted to the well-known theorem of Weil [1] which gives the construction of a group variety from a birational law. It seems that the generalization of this result to the case where the base is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring was already known to several people; see for example [2]. Here we shall prove the theorem for a group which is flat and of finite presentation over an arbitrary base prescheme S. Since the hypotheses must be made with a little more care, the statement is not in the form given by Weil; but when S is the spectrum of a field one sees that the form given here is essentially equivalent to Weil's. We use the following suggestion of Grothendieck: Given a "group germ" X over a prescheme S, one should construct the group as the quotient of \(\mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{X}\) by a suitable equivalence relation.
1. "Reminders" on rational maps

Let X/S be a relative prescheme and \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{X}\) an open subset. We say that U is schematically dense in X relative to S, or that \(\mathrm{U} \hookrightarrow \mathrm{X}\) is relatively schematically dense, if for every base change \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) the open subset \(\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}'\) is schematically dense in \(\mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}'\). For the definition and the properties of this notion we refer to Exp. IX, § 4.∗

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.1

(i) A finite intersection, as well as a union of a nonempty family, of open subsets schematically dense relative to S is schematically dense relative to S.

(ii) If \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{X}\) is schematically dense relative to S and if \(\mathrm{S} \to \mathrm{T}\) is a morphism, then \(\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{T}} \subseteq \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{T}}\) is schematically dense relative to T.

(iii) Let \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{V} \subseteq \mathrm{X}\) be open immersions. In order for U to be schematically dense in X relative to S, it is necessary and sufficient that it be so in V and that V be so in X.

(iv) If \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{X}\) and \(\mathrm{V} \subseteq \mathrm{Y}\) are relatively schematically dense, then \(\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{V}\) is schematically dense in \(\mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{Y}\) relative to S.
In Exp. IX one finds the following criterion:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2

Let X/S be locally of finite presentation and flat and let U be an open subset of X. If for each \(s \in \mathrm{S}\) the fiber \(\mathrm{U}_s\) is schematically dense in \(\mathrm{X}_s\), then U is schematically dense in X relative to S.
In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3

If X/S is locally of finite presentation and flat, and if each fiber \(\mathrm{X}_s\) is "without embedded component", then U is schematically dense in X relative to S if and only if for each \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm{U}_s\) is dense in \(\mathrm{X}_s\) in the topological sense.
One easily deduces from the definition the following

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.4

Let \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{X}\) be schematically dense relative to S and let \(f, g : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{Y}\) be two morphisms of functors over S, where Y satisfies one of the following conditions:

(i) Y is a prescheme over S and each fiber \(\mathrm{Y}_s\) is separated.

(ii) Y is a presheaf1 on S such that the diagonal morphism \(\mathrm{Y} \to \mathrm{Y} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{Y}\) is representable by a closed immersion.2

Then if \(f = g\) on U, we have \(f = g\).

Proof. In case (i), the standard argument following IX 4.1 shows that \(f\) and \(g\) are equal on each fiber, hence the subprescheme \(\mathrm{Ker}(f, g)\) of X is set-theoretically equal to X, hence is closed, and since it dominates U, it is equal to X, i.e. \(f = g\). Case (ii) is proved as in loc. cit.3

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.5

∗ Let X be a prescheme over S and Y a presheaf on S. A rational map \(f : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{Y}\) over S is an equivalence class of morphisms \(f : \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{Y}\) over S, where U is an open subset of X which is schematically dense relative to S, and where we set \((f : \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{Y}) \sim (f' : \mathrm{U}' \to \mathrm{Y})\) if and only if there exists an open subset \(\mathrm{U}'' \subseteq \mathrm{U}' \cap \mathrm{U}\), schematically dense relative to S, such that \(f = f'\) on \(\mathrm{U}''\).
This definition is made in such a way that one can define in an obvious fashion the rational map \(f \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}'\) for every base extension \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\).
If U is an open subset of X, we say that the rational map \(f\) is defined on U if there exists a morphism representing \(f\) whose set of definition contains U.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.5.1

4 If Y satisfies one of the conditions (i), (ii) of 1.4, it is clear that there exists a largest open subset U of X such that \(f\) is defined on U, and this U is schematically dense relative to S. It is called the domain of definition of \(f\) over S, and will be denoted \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
This notion does not commute with base change, but we have

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.6

Let X be an S-prescheme and Y an S-functor satisfying one of the hypotheses (i), (ii) of 1.4. Let \(f : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{Y}\) be a rational map over S, \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) a morphism which is flat and locally of finite presentation, and \(f' = f \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}'\). Then \[ \mathrm{Dom}(f') = \mathrm{Dom}(f) \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}'. \]

Proof. Set \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{Dom}(f)\). It is clear that \(\mathrm{V}' = \mathrm{Dom}(f')\) contains \(\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}'\). Let V be the image of \(\mathrm{V}'\), which is an open subset of X because \(\mathrm{X}' \to \mathrm{X}\) is open. We must show that \(\mathrm{V} = \mathrm{U}\), that is, we must find a morphism \(\mathrm{V} \to \mathrm{Y}\) which represents \(f\). Set \[ \mathrm{S}'' = \mathrm{S}' \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}', \qquad \mathrm{X}'' = \mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}'', \qquad \mathrm{U}'' = \mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}'', \qquad \mathrm{V}'' = \mathrm{V}' \times_{\mathrm{V}} \mathrm{V}'. \] Then \(\mathrm{U}''\) is schematically dense in \(\mathrm{X}''\) relative to \(\mathrm{S}''\), hence \(\mathrm{U}''\) is schematically dense in \(\mathrm{V}''\) relative to S, since \(\mathrm{U}'' \subseteq \mathrm{V}'' \subseteq \mathrm{X}''\). The restriction of \(f' : \mathrm{V}' \to \mathrm{Y}'\) to \(\mathrm{U}'\) is deduced from \(f : \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{Y}\) by base change. The two morphisms \(\mathrm{V}'' \to \mathrm{Y}\) deduced from \(g\) by base change are equal on \(\mathrm{U}''\), hence are equal. Now since \(\mathrm{V}' \to \mathrm{V}\) is flat and locally of finite presentation, it is fppf-covering (Exp. IV 6.3) and one obtains the morphism \(\mathrm{V} \to \mathrm{Y}\) by descent.
We shall make frequent use of the following triviality:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.7

Let X/S be faithfully flat, locally of finite presentation, and let \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{X}\) be a relatively schematically dense open subset. Then there exists a base extension \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) which is fppf-covering, and a section \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) which is contained in \(\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}')\).
Indeed, U/S is fppf-covering. We take \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathrm{U}\) and take as section the S-graph of the inclusion of U in X.

footnotes.html: (∗) cf. also EGA IV3, 11.9 and 11.10 (in particular 11.10.8), where one says "universally schematically dense relative to S".

(2) N.D.E.: Let us recall here the definition of a closed immersion. A morphism of S-functors \(\mathrm{F} \to \mathrm{G}\) is relatively representable if for every S-morphism \(\mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G}\), where T is an S-prescheme, the T-functor \(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{T}} = \mathrm{F} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{T}\) is representable by a T-prescheme. A morphism of S-functors \(\mathrm{F} \to \mathrm{G}\) is an open (resp. closed) immersion if it is relatively representable and if for every S-morphism \(\mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G}\), where T is an S-prescheme, the T-morphism \(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{T}} \to \mathrm{T}\) is an open (resp. closed) immersion.

pages.title: 2. Local determination of a group morphism

blocks.html: 2. Local determination of a group morphism

Let G and H be groups and let \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{G}\) be a subset such that \(\mathrm{U} \cdot \mathrm{U} = \mathrm{G}\). Then if \(f\) and \(g\) are homomorphisms from G to H such that \(f = g\) on U, we have \(f = g\). Similarly:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.1

Let S be a site (cf. Exp. IV5), G a sheaf of groups on S, and \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{G}\) a subsheaf of sets such that the morphism \(\mathrm{U} \times \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{G}\) induced by the multiplication is an epimorphism. Then if \(f\) and \(g\) are homomorphisms from G to a sheaf of groups H which are equal on U, we have \(f = g\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2

Let G be an S-group prescheme which is locally of finite presentation and flat, let \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{G}\) be an open subset which is relatively schematically dense, and H a sheaf of groups on S for the fppf topology. Then every homomorphism \(f : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) is determined by its restriction to U.

Indeed, since G is flat over S, the composition law of G is a flat morphism (VIB 9.2.xi) and it follows that \(\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{G}\) is faithfully flat and locally of finite presentation, hence fppf-covering, hence an epimorphism.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3

Let G be a group prescheme which is locally of finite presentation and flat over S, \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{G}\) a relatively schematically dense open subset, and H a sheaf of groups for the fppf topology. We denote by \(m_{\mathrm{G}}\) the multiplication of G and by \(m_{\mathrm{H}}\) that of H.

Let \(\overline{f} : \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{H}\) be an S-morphism and suppose that there exists a relatively schematically dense open subset V of \(m_{\mathrm{G}}^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \cap \mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U}\) such that the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{V} \ar[r]^-{(\overline{f} \times \overline{f})|_{\mathrm{V}}} \ar[d]_-{m_{\mathrm{G}}} & \mathrm{H} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{H} \ar[d]^-{m_{\mathrm{H}}} \\ \mathrm{U} \ar[r]_-{\overline{f}} & \mathrm{H} } \] is commutative. Then there exists a morphism of S-groups \(f : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) (necessarily unique) extending \(\overline{f}\), in each of the following situations:

(i) H is representable.

(ii) The diagonal morphism \(\mathrm{H} \to \mathrm{H} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{H}\) is representable by a closed immersion.6

(iii) For each section \(a \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S})\), the open subset \(\mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U}) \cap \mathrm{V}\big)\) is relatively schematically dense in U∗, and this statement remains true after every base change \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\).

Proof. Note first that V is relatively schematically dense in \(\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G}\). Indeed, V is relatively schematically dense in \[ m_{\mathrm{G}}^{-1}(\mathrm{U}) \cap (\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G}) \cap (\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U}) \] and the three factors are deduced from \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{G}\) by an obvious base change \(\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\), which implies the assertion by 1.1.

To construct a morphism \(f : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{H}\) it suffices, since \(\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{G}\) is fppf-covering, to find a morphism from \(\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U}\) to H such that the two induced morphisms on \((\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U}) \times_{\mathrm{G}} (\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U})\) are the same. Take \(m_{\mathrm{H}} \circ (\overline{f} \times \overline{f}) : \mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{H}\). One must verify that whenever we have sections \(a, b, c, d \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) arbitrary, such that \(ab = cd\), we also have \(\overline{f}(a)\overline{f}(b) = \overline{f}(c)\overline{f}(d)\). By hypothesis, this is true if \((a, b)\) and \((c, d)\) are contained in \(\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{S}')\), since in that case we have \(\overline{f}(a)\overline{f}(b) = \overline{f}(ab)\) and \(\overline{f}(c)\overline{f}(d) = \overline{f}(cd)\). Hence, it is true whenever \((a, b, c, d) \in (\mathrm{V} \times_{\mathrm{G}} \mathrm{V})(\mathrm{S}')\).

I claim that \(\mathrm{V} \times_{\mathrm{G}} \mathrm{V}\) is an open subset of \((\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U}) \times_{\mathrm{G}} (\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U})\) which is schematically dense relative to S. This will complete the proof in cases (i) and (ii) by 1.4, the facts that the morphism \(f\) thus constructed extends \(\overline{f}\) and that \(f\) is a homomorphism being obvious, again by 1.4. Now, write \[ \mathrm{V} \underset{\mathrm{G}}{\times} \mathrm{V} = \mathrm{V} \underset{\mathrm{G} \times \mathrm{G}}{\times} (\mathrm{G} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{G}) \underset{\mathrm{G}}{\times} \mathrm{V}. \] By symmetry and 1.1, it suffices to verify that \(\mathrm{V} \times_{\mathrm{G}} (\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G})\) is relatively schematically dense in \((\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G}) \times_{\mathrm{G}} (\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G})\). But this last prescheme is S-isomorphic to \(\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G}\), the morphism being given by \((a, b, c, d) \mapsto (a, b, c)\). Hence what must be shown is that \(\mathrm{V} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G}\) is schematically dense in \(\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G}\) relative to S, which is a consequence of the fact that V is relatively schematically dense in \(\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G}\).

It remains to treat case (iii). To prove \(\overline{f}(a)\overline{f}(b) = \overline{f}(c)\overline{f}(d)\), it is permissible to make an fppf-covering base extension. Suppose we have a section \(x \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) fppf-covering, such that \((b, x)\), \((a, bx)\), \((d, x)\), \((c, dx)\) all lie in V. Then we will have \[ \overline{f}(a)\overline{f}(b)\overline{f}(x) = \overline{f}(a)\overline{f}(bx) = \overline{f}(abx) = \overline{f}(cdx) = \overline{f}(c)\overline{f}(dx) = \overline{f}(c)\overline{f}(d)\overline{f}(x), \] whence the desired equality.7 To find such an \(x\), set, for every \(z \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}')\), \[ \mathrm{V}_z = \mathrm{pr}_2\big(z \times_{\mathrm{S}'} \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{S}'} \cap \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{S}'}\big). \] Then, the hypotheses on \(x\) mean that \(x \in \mathrm{W}\), where \[ \mathrm{W} = \mathrm{V}_b(\mathrm{S}') \cap \mathrm{V}_d(\mathrm{S}') \cap b^{-1}\mathrm{V}_a(\mathrm{S}') \cap d^{-1}\mathrm{V}_c(\mathrm{S}'). \] By (iii), W is relatively schematically dense in G. Hence the existence of a section \(x\) after an fppf-covering extension follows from Proposition 1.7.

Let us verify that the morphism thus constructed is multiplicative: Let \(a, b \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) arbitrary, and rename \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathrm{S}\). Choose an fppf-covering base extension \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and a section \(x \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \(x \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}')\) and \(ax^{-1} \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}')\), that is, \(x \in (a^{-1}\mathrm{U})^{-1}(\mathrm{S}')\). Choose moreover another fppf-covering extension \(\mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{S}'\) and a section \(y \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}'')\) such that \(y\), \(by^{-1}\), \(aby^{-1}\) belong to \(\mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}'')\), and \(by^{-1} \in \mathrm{V}_x\), \(xby^{-1} \in \mathrm{V}_{ax^{-1}}\). Then by the definition of \(f\), we have over \(\mathrm{S}''\) \[ f(a) = \overline{f}(ax^{-1})\overline{f}(x), \qquad f(b) = \overline{f}(by^{-1})\overline{f}(y), \qquad f(ab) = \overline{f}(aby^{-1})\overline{f}(y). \] Moreover, \[ f(a)f(b) = \overline{f}(ax^{-1})\overline{f}(x)\overline{f}(by^{-1})\overline{f}(y) = \overline{f}(ax^{-1})\overline{f}(xby^{-1})\overline{f}(y) = \overline{f}(aby^{-1})\overline{f}(y) = f(ab), \] whence the multiplicativity.

The fact that \(f\) extends \(\overline{f}\) is now easy: Let \(a \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}')\), rename \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathrm{S}\), and choose \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) fppf-covering and sections \(x, y \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \((x, y)\), \((ax, y)\), \((a, xy)\) are in \(\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{S}')\). Then \[ f(xy) = \overline{f}(x)\overline{f}(y) = \overline{f}(xy), \qquad f(axy) = \overline{f}(ax)\overline{f}(y) = \overline{f}(axy). \] Hence \[ f(a) = f(axy)f\big((xy)^{-1}\big) = f(axy)f(xy)^{-1} = \overline{f}(axy)\overline{f}(xy)^{-1} = \overline{f}(a). \]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.3.1

In many cases hypothesis (iii) is true, for it will even be true if one replaces U by a smaller open subset \(\mathrm{U}'\), still relatively schematically dense in G. For example we have:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4

With the situation as in 2.3, suppose that every geometric fiber of G/S is irreducible. Let \(\mathrm{U}' = \mathrm{pr}_1 \mathrm{V}\) and \[ \mathrm{V}' = \mathrm{V} \cap m_{\mathrm{G}}^{-1}(\mathrm{U}') \cap (\mathrm{U}' \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{U}'). \] Then \(\mathrm{U}' \subseteq \mathrm{U}\) is a relatively schematically dense open subset of G and the objects \(\mathrm{U}'\), \(\overline{f}|_{\mathrm{U}'}\) and \(\mathrm{V}'\) satisfy hypothesis (iii).

Proof. \(\mathrm{U}'\) is open because \(\mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}\) is flat and locally of finite presentation. All the other verifications are trivial except hypothesis (iii). Let \(a \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}')\), rename \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathrm{S}\). To verify that \(\mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{U}') \cap \mathrm{V}'\big)\) is relatively schematically dense in \(\mathrm{U}'\), it suffices to do so fiber by fiber by Corollary 1.3, that is, it suffices to treat the case where S is the spectrum of a field, and in that case it suffices to show that \(\mathrm{U}'\) is nonempty, because G is irreducible and "without embedded components" (cf. VIA, 1.1.1). Now \[ \mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{U}') \cap \mathrm{V}'\big) = \mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{U}') \cap \mathrm{V}\big) \cap \mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{U}') \cap m_{\mathrm{G}}^{-1}(\mathrm{U}')\big) \] and the second term of the right-hand side is dense in G. Hence it suffices to show that \(\mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{U}') \cap \mathrm{V}\big)\) is dense in G, that is, nonempty, which is clear since \(a \in \mathrm{U}' = \mathrm{pr}_1 \mathrm{V}\).

pages.title: 3. Construction of a group from a rational law

blocks.html: 3. Construction of a group from a rational law

We suppose given a prescheme X/S and a rational map \(\mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{X}\) over S, and we look for a group G/S and a birational map relative to S8 \[ \mathrm{X} \dashrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] which commutes with the composition laws. We treat only the case where X/S satisfies the following hypothesis:
(♦) X/S is faithfully flat, of finite presentation, and with separated fibers "without embedded components".
(Note that the last two hypotheses are properties which hold for a group prescheme9). We shall often suppress the symbol S in fibered products.
Let X/S be a prescheme having the properties (♦) above and let W be a subprescheme of finite presentation of \(\mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}\) having the following property:

(∗) The three morphisms \(\mathrm{W} \to \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}\) given by the projections of \(\mathrm{X}^3\) onto \(\mathrm{X}^2\) are open immersions which are schematically dense relative to S.

blocks.title: Notations
blocks.html:
Notations

We shall use the following terminology: Given sections \(a, b, c \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) arbitrary, such that \((a, b, c) \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\), we write: \[ c = ab, \qquad b = a^{-1}c, \qquad a = cb^{-1}. \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.0.1

10 Given a section \((a, b) \in \mathrm{X}^2(\mathrm{S}')\), we say that \(ab\) is defined if, and only if, there exists a section \(c \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \((a, b, c) \in \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\), i.e. if and only if \((a, b)\) lies in \(\mathrm{pr}_{12} \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\). Likewise, saying that \(a^{-1}b\) or \(ab^{-1}\) is defined has the analogous meaning, and we extend this terminology also to products of several factors.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.0.2

Let us note right away the following fact: By (i), W defines a rational map \(\mathrm{X}^2 \to \mathrm{X}\) over S (the one given by \((a, b) \mapsto ab\)). It may well happen that this rational map has a domain of definition larger than \(\mathrm{pr}_{12} \mathrm{W}\). Nevertheless, we say that \(ab\) is defined only if \((a, b) \in \mathrm{pr}_{12} \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{S}')\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1

A group germ is a prescheme X/S having the properties (♦) above together with a subprescheme of finite presentation W of \(\mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}\) having the following properties:

(i) W satisfies property (∗) above.

(ii) For each section \(a \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S})\), the sets \[ \mathrm{pr}_i\big((a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}) \cap \mathrm{W}\big), \quad i = 2, 3, \] \[ \mathrm{pr}_i\big((\mathrm{X} \times a \times \mathrm{X}) \cap \mathrm{W}\big), \quad i = 3, 1, \] \[ \mathrm{pr}_i\big((\mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X} \times a) \cap \mathrm{W}\big), \quad i = 1, 2, \] are schematically dense in X relative to S, and this statement remains true after every base change \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\). (Hypothesis (i) implies that these sets are open subsets of X.) Intuitively, this means that "\(ax\) is defined for \(x\) sufficiently general", etc.

(iii) The law is associative, that is, if \((a, b, c) \in \mathrm{X}^3(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) arbitrary, is such that \((ab)c\) and \(a(bc)\) are defined, we have \((ab)c = a(bc)\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks

(a) In (i) one may replace the condition that W be schematically dense in \(\mathrm{X}^2\) relative to S by the condition that for each \(s \in \mathrm{S}\) the fiber \(\mathrm{W}_s\) is dense in \(\mathrm{X}^2_s\) in the topological sense, thanks to the hypotheses made on X and to 1.2.

(b) Condition (iii) is equivalent to the following: Let \(\mathrm{V}_1\) (resp. \(\mathrm{V}_2\)) be the open subset of \(\mathrm{X}^3\) where the rational map \((a, b, c) \mapsto a(bc)\) (resp. \((a, b, c) \mapsto (ab)c\)) is defined∗. Then, there exists an open subset \(\mathrm{V} \subseteq \mathrm{V}_1 \cap \mathrm{V}_2\) which is schematically dense in \(\mathrm{X}^3\) relative to S such that the two preceding maps coincide on V. This is a consequence of 1.4 because, of course, \(\mathrm{V}_1\) and \(\mathrm{V}_2\) are schematically dense in \(\mathrm{X}^3\) relative to S.

(c) Hypothesis (ii) will serve below to ensure that X will be a subobject of the group prescheme it defines. In many cases one can deduce (ii) from (i), provided one replaces X by a relatively schematically dense open subset \(\mathrm{X}'\), and W by a relatively schematically dense open subset of \(\mathrm{W} \cap \mathrm{X}'^3\). In fact we have:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.2

Suppose that each geometric fiber of X/S is irreducible and let W be a subprescheme of \(\mathrm{X}^3\) which satisfies condition (i) of 3.1. Then there exist a relatively schematically dense open subset \(\mathrm{X}'\) of X and a relatively schematically dense open subset \(\mathrm{W}'\) of \(\mathrm{W} \cap (\mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X}')\) such that the pair \((\mathrm{X}', \mathrm{W}')\) satisfies conditions (i) and (ii). If (iii) holds for \((\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{W})\), it holds for \((\mathrm{X}', \mathrm{W}')\).

Proof. Set \(\mathrm{X}' = \bigcap_{i=1}^{3} \mathrm{pr}_i \mathrm{W}\). Each \(\mathrm{pr}_i \mathrm{W}\) is open in X because \(\mathrm{W} \to \mathrm{X}^2\) is an open immersion and the projections \(\mathrm{X}^2 \to \mathrm{X}\) are flat and of finite presentation. Moreover, \(\mathrm{pr}_i \mathrm{W}\) is relatively schematically dense in X because W is so in \(\mathrm{X}^2\) and the projections \(\mathrm{X}^2 \to \mathrm{X}\) are surjective (it suffices to verify density in the topological sense). Take \(\mathrm{W}' = \mathrm{W} \cap \mathrm{X}'^3\).

To verify that (i) holds, note that \[ \mathrm{W}' = \big(\mathrm{W} \cap (\mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X})\big) \cap \big(\mathrm{W} \cap (\mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X}')\big) \cap \big(\mathrm{W} \cap (\mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}')\big). \] Now \(\mathrm{W} \cap (\mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X}) \simeq \mathrm{pr}_{12} \mathrm{W} \cap \mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X}'\), hence is relatively schematically dense in W. Likewise, the other terms of the right-hand side are relatively schematically dense in W, and consequently \(\mathrm{W}'\) is relatively schematically dense in W. Hence \(\mathrm{pr}_{ij} \mathrm{W}'\) is relatively schematically dense in \(\mathrm{pr}_{ij} \mathrm{W}\), hence in \(\mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}\), hence in \(\mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X}'\).

To verify condition (ii), let \(a \in \mathrm{X}'(\mathrm{S}')\), and rename \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathrm{S}\). We must show that, for example, \(\mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X}') \cap \mathrm{W}\big)\) is schematically dense in \(\mathrm{X}'\) relative to S. By 1.3, it suffices to verify this fiber by fiber, that is, it suffices to treat the case where S is the spectrum of a field, and in that case, it suffices to verify that the open set is nonempty, because the fibers of X/S are irreducible and "without embedded components". Since \(\mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}) \cap \mathrm{W}\big)\) is nonempty, \(a\) being a section of \(\mathrm{X}'\), this open set is dense in X. We have \[ \mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}) \cap \mathrm{W}\big) = \mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{X}) \cap \mathrm{pr}_{12} \mathrm{W}\big). \] Hence, \[ \mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{X}) \cap \mathrm{pr}_{12} \mathrm{W}\big) \cap \mathrm{X}' = \mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{X}') \cap \mathrm{pr}_{12} \mathrm{W}\big) = \mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X}) \cap \mathrm{W}\big) \] is dense in X, hence in \(\mathrm{X}'\).

Similarly, \(\mathrm{pr}_3(a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}' \cap \mathrm{W})\) is dense in X, hence in \(\mathrm{pr}_3(a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X} \cap \mathrm{W})\), that is, \((a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}' \cap \mathrm{W})\) is dense in \((a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X} \cap \mathrm{W})\), that is, \(\mathrm{pr}_2(a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}' \cap \mathrm{W})\) is dense in \(\mathrm{pr}_2(a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X} \cap \mathrm{W})\), hence dense in X, hence dense in \(\mathrm{X}'\). Now since \[ \mathrm{pr}_2(a \times \mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X}' \cap \mathrm{W}') = \mathrm{pr}_2(a \times \mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X}' \cap \mathrm{W}) = \mathrm{pr}_2(a \times \mathrm{X}' \times \mathrm{X} \cap \mathrm{W}) \cap \mathrm{pr}_2(a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}' \cap \mathrm{W}), \] it is indeed dense in \(\mathrm{X}'\), which is what had to be shown. The other assertions of (ii) follow by symmetry, and the fact that condition (iii) is preserved is trivial.11 Proposition 3.2 is proved.12

Let us now fix a group germ \((\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{W})\) over S. We must make some preliminary remarks about the situation, which we have collected below. We shall often use these rules without explicit mention in what follows.

Let \(a \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\), and rename \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathrm{S}\). Then one obtains a (bi)rational map \(\varphi\) over S from X to itself by assigning to a section \(x\) the section \(ax\) when it is defined. By 3.1 (ii), the domain of definition of \(\varphi\) contains the relatively schematically dense open subset \(\mathrm{pr}_2\big((a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}) \cap \mathrm{W}\big)\), and \(\varphi\) defines an isomorphism from this open subset onto the open subset (where \(a^{-1}x\) is defined) \(\mathrm{pr}_3\big((a \times \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}) \cap \mathrm{W}\big)\). This remark is generalized in the obvious way in Rule 1.

blocks.title: Rule 1
blocks.html:
Rule 1

Let \(\mathrm{P} = \mathrm{P}(x, t_1, \ldots, t_n)\) be a "product" of the symbols \(x, t_1, \ldots, t_n\) obtained recursively in the following manner: \(\mathrm{P}_0 = x\); \(\mathrm{P}_{i+1} = \) one of the following expressions: \[ \mathrm{P}_i t, \quad t \mathrm{P}_i, \quad \mathrm{P}_i^{-1} t, \quad t \mathrm{P}_i^{-1}, \quad \mathrm{P}_i t^{-1}, \quad t^{-1} \mathrm{P}_i, \] where \(t\) is one of the \(t_j\); \(\mathrm{P}_r = \mathrm{P}\). Let \(a_1, \ldots, a_n \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\). Then there exists a relatively schematically dense open subset U of \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) such that the product \(\mathrm{P}(x, a_1, \ldots, a_n)\) is defined (in the sense of Remark 3.0.2) for a section \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}'')\) if and only if \(x \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}'')\), and the map \(x \to \mathrm{P}(x, (a))\) gives an isomorphism from U onto another relatively schematically dense open subset, denoted \(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{U}, (a))\), of \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}'}\).

blocks.title: Rule 2
blocks.html:
Rule 2

Let \(a, b \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\). Then:

If \(ab\) is defined, so is \(a^{-1}(ab)\), and \(a^{-1}(ab) = b\).

If \(a^{-1}b\) is defined, so is \(a(a^{-1}b)\), and \(a(a^{-1}b) = b\).

If \(ba^{-1}\) is defined, so is \((ba^{-1})a\), and \((ba^{-1})a = b\).

blocks.title: Rule 3
blocks.html:
Rule 3

Let \(a, b, b' \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\). If \(ab = ab'\), if \(ba = b'a\), if \(a^{-1}b = a^{-1}b'\) or if \(ba^{-1} = b'a^{-1}\), then \(b = b'\). Here it is understood that the equality relation implies that both sides are defined.

blocks.title: Rule 4
blocks.html:
Rule 4

Let \(a, b, c \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\). Then whenever both sides are defined, we have \[ a\big((ba)^{-1}c\big) = b^{-1}c. \] Similarly: \[ \big(c(ab)^{-1}\big)a = cb^{-1}, \qquad a^{-1}\big((ab^{-1})c\big) = b^{-1}c, \qquad \big(c(b^{-1}a)\big)a^{-1} = cb^{-1}. \]

blocks.title: Rule 5
blocks.html:
Rule 5

All the following associativity laws hold, whenever both sides are defined: \[ (a^{-1}b)c = a^{-1}(bc), \qquad (ab^{-1})c = a(b^{-1}c), \qquad (ab)c^{-1} = a(bc^{-1}), \] \[ (ab)^{-1}c = b^{-1}(a^{-1}c), \qquad (a^{-1}b)c^{-1} = a^{-1}(bc^{-1}), \qquad (ab^{-1})c^{-1} = a(cb)^{-1}. \]
3.2.2. Verification of the rules.
(1) is done by an obvious induction on the length \(r\) of P, the case \(r = 1\) being a direct consequence of (3.1) (ii).
(2) This is trivial from the definition.
(3) Indeed, by Rule 2, for example in the first case, we have \(b = a^{-1}(ab) = a^{-1}(ab') = b'\).

(4) Let us verify for example the first relation: on the right-hand side left multiplication by \(b\) is defined and gives \(c\), by Rule 2. Suppose it is also defined on the left-hand side. Then we will have \[ b\Big(a\big((ba)^{-1}c\big)\Big) = (ba)\big((ba)^{-1}c\big) = c. \] Indeed \((ba)\) is defined by hypothesis because it appears in the expression. Hence the middle member is defined and equal to \(c\) by Rule 2, and equal to the left member by associativity (cf. 3.1 (iii)). Hence Rule 3 implies that the desired equality holds if this multiplication by \(b\) is defined.

Now, fix \(a\) and \(b\). Then Rule 1 implies that \(b\big(a((ba)^{-1}c)\big)\) is indeed defined for \(c\) "in" a relatively schematically dense open subset U of X, so on this relatively schematically dense open subset the two rational maps \(c \mapsto b^{-1}c\) and \(c \mapsto a\big((ba)^{-1}c\big)\) are equal. By 1.4, they are equal on every common domain of definition, whence the desired result.

(5) This is the same kind of reasoning as the preceding one. For example one verifies \((ab^{-1})c^{-1} = a(cb)^{-1}\) in the following manner: If right multiplication by \(c\) is defined on the right-hand side, we have equality by Rule 4. Since it suffices to verify such a formula on a relatively schematically dense open subset, one reduces to the case where this multiplication is indeed defined.

Let us now consider the relation R on \(\mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X}\) obtained by setting, for \(a, b, a', b' \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\), \((a, b) \sim (a', b')\) modulo \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{S}')\) if, and only if, there exist an \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) covering for fppf and a section \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \((xa)b\) and \((xa')b'\) are defined and equal. Then R is an equivalence relation.

Indeed, this relation is obviously symmetric. By Rule 1, the product \((xa)b\) is defined if \(x\) is "in" a suitable relatively schematically dense open subset. Hence 1.7 asserts that there exist an \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) covering for fppf and an \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \((xa)b\) is defined. The relation is therefore reflexive, and transitivity is a consequence of the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3

Let \(x, y, a, b, a', b'\), be sections of \(\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \((xa)b\), \((xa')b'\), \((ya)b\), \((ya')b'\) are defined. If \((xa)b = (xa')b'\) then \((ya)b = (ya')b'\).

Indeed, the lemma says that one can test \((a, b) \sim (a', b')\) with an arbitrary \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) fppf-covering, such that the two products are defined. Given \(a, b, a', b', a'', b'' \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S})\), one can, by Rule 1, 1.1 and 1.7, find an fppf-covering extension \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and a section \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that the three products in question are defined, whence transitivity.

Proof of the lemma. Let us write formally to begin with: \[ (za)b = \big(((zx^{-1})x)a\big)b = \big((zx^{-1})(xa)\big)b = (zx^{-1})\big((xa)b\big) \] \[ (za')b' = \big(((zx^{-1})x)a'\big)b' = \big((zx^{-1})(xa')\big)b' = (zx^{-1})\big((xa')b'\big) \] One verifies that these equalities are indeed true if the members are defined, by the appropriate rules13. It follows that \((za)b = (za')b'\) if all these expressions are defined. Moreover, by Rule 1 and the hypotheses already made, these expressions are indeed defined if \(z \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}'')\) is in \(\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{S}'')\), where V is a certain relatively schematically dense open subset of X (we have taken \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathrm{S}\)). Hence the two rational maps from X to itself given by \(z \mapsto (za)b\) and \(z \mapsto (za')b'\) are equal, whence \((ya)b = (ya')b'\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.4

Consider the rational map \(\varphi : \mathrm{X}^3 \to \mathrm{X}\) over S defined by \((a, b, c) \mapsto c^{-1}(ab)\). Let U be the domain of definition of \(\varphi\) and consider the graph \(\Gamma\) of the morphism \(f : \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{X}\) induced by \(\varphi\), which is a subscheme of \(\mathrm{X}^4\). Then a section \((a, b, c, d) \in \mathrm{X}^4(\mathrm{S})\) is in \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S})\) if and only if \((a, b) \sim (c, d)\).

Proof. Note first that the rational map \(\varphi\) is the same as the one given by the formula \((a, b, c) \mapsto (xc)^{-1}((xa)b)\) for an arbitrary section \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S})\). This amounts to saying that we have \(c^{-1}(ab) = (xc)^{-1}((xa)b)\) whenever both sides are defined. We leave the verification to the reader.

Let us then show that the map \(\varphi\) is defined at a section \((a, b, c) \in \mathrm{X}^3(\mathrm{S})\) if and only if there exists \(d\) with \((a, b) \simeq (c, d)\), and that then \(d = \varphi(a, b, c)\). Indeed suppose we have \((a, b) \sim (c, d)\). To verify that the map \(\varphi\) is defined, it is permissible to make an fppf-covering base extension (Proposition 1.6), and we may therefore suppose that there exists a section \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \((xa)b\) and \((xc)d\) are defined and equal. It follows (Rule 2) that \((xc)^{-1}((xa)b)\) is defined and equal to \(d\).

Conversely, suppose the map \(\varphi\) defined at the section \((a, b, c)\) and let \(d = \varphi(a, b, c)\). Choose an fppf-covering \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and a section \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) such that \((xa)b\) and \((xc)d\) are defined. We want to show that they are equal. For this, it suffices to show that the two rational maps over S from X to itself, given by \(b \mapsto (xa)b\) and \(b \mapsto (xc)\varphi(a, b, c)\) are the same, which follows from the remark of the first paragraph.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.5

The equivalence relation \(\mathrm{R} \rightrightarrows \mathrm{X}^2\) is representable and it is a flat relation of finite presentation, that is, the projections of R onto \(\mathrm{X}^2\) are flat morphisms of finite presentation.

Proof. We may suppose S affine. Since \((\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{W})\) is of finite presentation over S one can descend the whole situation to an S of finite type over \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Z}\), hence noetherian. We may therefore suppose that S is noetherian. Then it is trivial that the graph \(\Gamma\) of (3.4) is of finite presentation over \(\mathrm{X}^2\). The projection \(\mathrm{pr}_{12}|\Gamma\) is flat because \(\Gamma \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{pr}_{123} \Gamma\), which is an open subset of \(\mathrm{X}^3\), and the projection \(\mathrm{pr}_{12} : \mathrm{X}^3 \to \mathrm{X}^2\) is flat because X is flat over S.

I claim that \(\Gamma\) represents R. Note that there is something to prove because the domain of definition of a rational map does not in general commute with base extensions. Let \(\mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{S}\). What is clear, by (3.4) applied to \(\mathrm{S}''\), is that \(\mathrm{R}(\mathrm{S}'') \supset \Gamma(\mathrm{S}'')\), because \(\varphi \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}''\) is certainly defined on \(\mathrm{U} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}''\). So let \((a, b, c, d) \in \mathrm{R}(\mathrm{S}'')\). We must show that \((a, b, c, d) \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}'')\). The verification of this is done locally for, say, the étale topology. We may therefore suppose \(\mathrm{S}''\) strictly local, i.e., the spectrum of a henselian ring with separably closed residue field. Moreover, applying (1.6) and the usual limit-passage sorites, one reduces to the case S strictly local and \(\mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{S}\) local. Suppose we have a section \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S})\) such that over \(\mathrm{S}''\) the products \((xa)b\) and \((xc)d\) are defined. This will imply that \((xc)^{-1}((xa)b)\) is defined, and equal to \(d\). Now there exists a relatively sch. dense open subset V of \(\mathrm{X}^3\) such that \((xc)^{-1}((xa)b)\) is defined if and only if \((a, b, c) \in \mathrm{V}(\mathrm{S}'')\), and we have \(\mathrm{V} \subseteq \mathrm{U}\). Hence \((a, b, c, d) \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}'')\) if such an \(x\) exists. By (1.6) it is permissible to make an fppf-covering base extension \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) in order to find such an \(x\). Since S is strictly local, one can∗ find an \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) faithfully flat, local, and finite and a section \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) which "passes through" an arbitrary closed point of the closed fiber of X/S. Now saying that \((xa)b\) and \((xc)d\) are defined means that over \(\mathrm{S}''\), \(x\) lies in a certain relatively sch. dense open subset, which is verified on the closed fiber of \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}''}\). So it works.

Now let G be the quotient of \(\mathrm{X}^2\) by R as a sheaf for the fppf topology. We shall define a composition law on G in the following manner: Let \((g, g') \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}')\) be represented by a section \(((a, b), (c, d))\) of \(\mathrm{X}^2 \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{X}^2(\mathrm{S}'')\), \(\mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{S}'\) fppf-covering. We suppose moreover that X admits a section \(x\) over \(\mathrm{S}''\) such that \(a(b(cx))\) and \(x^{-1}d\) are defined, which is permissible by Rule 1 and (1.7), and we call \(gg'\) the class in \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}')\) represented by the section \((a(b(cx)), x^{-1}d)\) of \(\mathrm{X}^2(\mathrm{S}'')\).

Let us verify that \(gg'\) does not depend on the choice of the section \(x\) and of the representative \(((a, b), (c, d))\): Indeed let \((a', b') \sim (a, b)\), \((c', d') \sim (c, d)\), and \(x\) be such that \(a'(b'(c'x'))\) and \(x'^{-1}d'\) are defined. We may suppose that all are sections over \(\mathrm{S}''\). One must show that \[ (a(b(cx)), x^{-1}d) \sim (a'(b'(c'x')), x'^{-1}d'), \] that is, that for a suitable section \(z \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}''')\), \(\mathrm{S}''' \to \mathrm{S}''\) a suitable fppf-covering, we have \[ (z(a(b(cx))))(x^{-1}d) = (z(a'(b'(c'x'))))(x'^{-1}d'). \] Whenever all the products are defined, we have \[ \begin{aligned} (z(a(b(cx))))(x^{-1}d) &= ((za)(b(cx)))(x^{-1}d) = (((za)b)(cx))(x^{-1}d) \\ &= ((((za)b)c)x)(x^{-1}d) = (((za)b)c)(x(x^{-1}d)) = (((za)b)c)d, \end{aligned} \] and the same identities hold with the primes. Now, by Rule 1 and (1.7) such a \(z\) exists. One must therefore show that \((((za)b)c)d = (((za')b')c')d'\). But \((za)b = (za')b'\) because \((a, b) \sim (a', b')\) (3.3) and we thus have the desired equality because \((c, d) \sim (c', d')\).

Consider the natural morphism \(i : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) defined in the following manner: For \(a \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\), one chooses an fppf-covering \(\mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{S}'\) and a section \(b \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}'')\) such that \(ab^{-1}\) is defined, and one sets \[ i(a) = \text{class in G of } (ab^{-1}, b). \] One easily verifies that this class, which is a priori in \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}'')\), does not depend on the choice of \(b\) and therefore gives a well-determined element of \(\mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}')\).
The reader will take pleasure in verifying the

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.6

The morphism \(i\) commutes with the composition laws of X and of G, i.e., if \(a, b \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) are sections such that \(ab\) is defined, we have \(i(a)i(b) = i(ab)\).
The aim of this section is the following theorem:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.7

14 Let \((\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{W})\) be a group germ over S, with X/S faithfully flat, of finite presentation, and with separated fibers without embedded component. Then with the notations above we have

(i) G is a sheaf of groups.

(ii) \(i : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) is representable by an open immersion.

(iii) G is representable locally over S for the fppf topology.

(iv) If G/S is representable, then it is a flat group of finite presentation, and \(i : \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) is schematically dense relative to S.
Note that G is obviously characterized by properties (i), …, (iv); one may therefore forget the explicit construction of G.
The proof proceeds in several steps:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.8

(i) Let \(c\) be a section of \(\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S})\); then the morphism from the prescheme \(c \times \mathrm{X}\) (which is S-isomorphic to X) to G given by \(c \times \mathrm{X} \hookrightarrow \mathrm{X} \times \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) is a monomorphism.

(ii) Let \(\{c_i\}\), \(i \in \mathrm{I}\), be sections of \(\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S})\), let \(\mathrm{Z} = \coprod_i c_i \times \mathrm{X}\), and call \(\mathrm{R}' \rightrightarrows \mathrm{Z}\) the equivalence relation induced on Z by the obvious morphism \(\mathrm{Z} \to \mathrm{X}^2\). Then \(\mathrm{R}'\) is a "gluing" of the \(c_i \times \mathrm{X} \simeq \mathrm{X}\).

Proof. (i) For two sections \((c, a)\) and \((c, a')\) of \((c \times \mathrm{X})(\mathrm{S}')\) to have the same image in G, one must have \((c, a) \sim (c, a')\), that is, \((xc)a \sim (xc)a'\) for suitable \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}'')\), \(\mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{S}'\) fppf-covering, whence \(a = a'\) by Rule 3.

(ii) Let \(c_i, c_j\) be two sections and consider the birational map \(\psi_{ji}\) from X to itself over S which is given by the formula \(x \mapsto c_j^{-1}(c_i x)\). It is the same map as the one given by \(x \mapsto (yc_j)^{-1}((yc_i)x)\), if \(y \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S})\), as one sees easily. Moreover, one verifies that \(\varphi_{ji}\) is defined at a \(b \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}')\) if and only if there exists \(b'\) such that \((c_i, b) \sim (c_j, b')\), and then \(b' = \varphi_{ji}(b)\). Let \(\mathrm{U}_{ji}\) be the domain of definition over S of \(\varphi_{ji}\). It remains to show that this domain of definition is universal, that is, that if \(b \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}'')\), \(\mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{S}\) arbitrary, and if \(\varphi_{ji}\) is defined at \(b\), then \(b \in \mathrm{U}_{ji}(\mathrm{S}'')\). This amounts to showing that if \(b, b' \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}'')\) are such that \((c_i, b) \simeq (c_j, b')\), then \(b \in \mathrm{U}_{ji}(\mathrm{S}'')\). We leave the verification of this fact, which is analogous to that of (3.5), to the reader.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.9

Suppose that \(\{c_i\}\), \(i \in \mathrm{I}\), are sections of \(\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S})\) such that \(\coprod_i c_i \times \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) is surjective as a morphism of sheaves. Then G is representable and flat, of finite presentation over S, and the structural morphism \(\mathrm{X}^2 \to \mathrm{G}\) is flat and of finite presentation.

Proof. The fact that G is representable is an immediate consequence of (3.8), and it follows that \(\coprod_i c_i \times \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) is an open covering. To show that \(\mathrm{X}^2 \to \mathrm{G}\) is flat, it suffices to do so locally, hence to show that the rational map \(\mathrm{X}^2 \to c_i \times \mathrm{X}\) induces a flat morphism on its domain of definition. Now this rational map is given by \((a, b) \mapsto (c_i, ((xc_i)^{-1}(xa))b)\), \(x\) an arbitrary section, and if it is defined at \((a, b) \in \mathrm{X}^2(\mathrm{S}')\), one can find an fppf-covering \(\mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{S}'\) and a section \(x \in \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S}'')\) such that \(((xc_i)^{-1}(xa))b\) is defined. One sees easily that it is therefore a flat morphism. It follows likewise that it is locally of finite presentation, hence fppf-covering. Now by construction, the relation R is effective. Hence by (3.5) the morphism \(\mathrm{X}^2 \to \mathrm{G}\) becomes of finite presentation after the base change \(\mathrm{G} \leftarrow \mathrm{X}^2\), which is fppf-covering, hence \(\mathrm{X}^2 \to \mathrm{G}\) is of finite presentation. Let us show that \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{S}\) is flat and of finite presentation. It is flat and locally of finite presentation since G is covered by the \(c_i \times \mathrm{X} \simeq \mathrm{X}\). Now \(\mathrm{X}^2/\mathrm{S}\) is quasi-compact, and \(\mathrm{X}^2 \to \mathrm{G}\) is surjective. This shows that G/S is quasi-compact. To show that \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{S}\) is quasi-separated, note that we have the following cartesian diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{R} \ar[r]^-{\alpha} \ar[d]_-{\gamma} & \mathrm{X}^2 \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{X}^2 \ar[d]^-{\beta} \\ \mathrm{G} \ar[r]_-{\Delta} & \mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{G}. } \] We have \(\gamma\) surjective and \(\alpha, \beta\) quasi-compact, hence \(\beta\alpha\) is quasi-compact, hence \(\Delta\) is quasi-compact.15

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.10

Let \(\{c_i\}\), \(i \in \mathrm{I}\) be sections of \(\mathrm{X}(\mathrm{S})\). For \(\coprod_i c_i \times \mathrm{X} \to \mathrm{G}\) to be surjective as a morphism of fppf-sheaves, it suffices that the following condition be satisfied:

For each \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and \((a, b) \in \mathrm{X}^2(\mathrm{S}')\), there exist an open covering \(\{\mathrm{S}'_\nu\}\), \(\nu \in \mathrm{N}\), of \(\mathrm{S}'\) and a function \(\mathrm{N} \to \mathrm{I}\) \((\nu \mapsto i(\nu))\) such that \((c_{i(\nu)}^{-1}a)b\) is defined over \(\mathrm{S}'_\nu\).

Proof. Let \(\mathrm{S}'' o \mathrm{S}\) be arbitrary, and \(g \in \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{S}'')\). Choose an fppf-covering \(\mathrm{S}' o \mathrm{S}''\) and a section \((a, b) \in \mathrm{X}^2(\mathrm{S}')\) which represents \(g\). Take the open covering \(\{\mathrm{S}'_ u\}\) of \(\mathrm{S}'\) which exists by the hypothesis of the lemma. Then over each \(\mathrm{S}'_ u\) we have \((a, b) \sim (c_{i( u)}, (c_{i( u)}^{-1}a)b)\), hence \(g\) is represented by a section of \([c_{i( u)} imes \mathrm{X}](\mathrm{S}')\) over \(\mathrm{S}'_ u\), which proves the surjectivity, because the family of morphisms \(\{\mathrm{S}'_ u o \mathrm{S}''\}\) is fppf-covering.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.11

Let Y/S be a prescheme of finite presentation, and let \(\{a_i\}\), \(i \in \mathrm{I}\), be sections of \(\mathrm{Y}(\mathrm{S})\). Let \(s_0, s_1\) be points of S such that \(s_0\) is a specialization of \(s_1\), and \(\mathrm{Y}_j\) the fiber of Y/S at the point \(s_j\). Let \(\mathrm{C}_j\) be the closure in \(\mathrm{Y}_j\) of the set of points \(\{a_i(s) \cap \mathrm{Y}_j\}\). Then we have \(\dim \mathrm{C}_1 \geqslant \dim \mathrm{C}_0\).

Proof. It suffices to make the verification after a base change \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) with chosen points \(s'_0\) and \(s'_1\) such that \(s'_j \mapsto s_j\) and \(s'_0\) is a specialization of \(s'_1\). One is therefore (EGA II 7.1.4) reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a valuation ring A, \(s_0\) the closed point of S, and \(s_1\) the generic point of S. Now let V be the closure of \(\mathrm{C}_1\) in Y. It is clear that \(\mathrm{C}_0 \subseteq \mathrm{V}\), and thus the lemma is a consequence of the "well-known" fact that an irreducible closed subprescheme V of a prescheme Y/S of finite presentation satisfies \(\dim \mathrm{V} \times s_1 \geqslant \dim \mathrm{V} \times s_0\) if S is the spectrum of a valuation ring and if \(\mathrm{V} \times s_1 \neq \varnothing\) (EGA IV 13.1.6).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.12

Suppose that S is the spectrum of a local ring, with closed point \(s_0\), and let \(\{c_i\}\), \(i \in \mathrm{I}\), be sections such that the closure \(\mathrm{C}_0\) of the set \(\{c_i(s) \cap \mathrm{X}_0\}\) in the closed fiber \(\mathrm{X}_0\) is of dimension equal to \(\dim \mathrm{X}_0 = n\). Then the condition of Lemma 3.9 is satisfied.

Proof. Note first that the fibers of X/S all have the same dimension \(u\), which results from EGA IV 12.1.1 (i) and from the fact that X has a rational composition law. Lemma (3.11) therefore implies that for each morphism \(\mathrm{S}_1 \to \mathrm{S}\) with \(\mathrm{S}_1\) the spectrum of a field, the dimension of the closure of the set \(\{c_i \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}_1\}\) in \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}_1}\) is equal to \(n\). Let us verify the condition of (3.10): Let \((a, b) \in \mathrm{X}^2(\mathrm{S}')\). For \((c_i^{-1}a)b\) to be defined, it is necessary and sufficient that \(c_i\) be contained in a certain open subset \(\mathrm{U} \subseteq \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) which is sch. dense, relative to \(\mathrm{S}'\) (Rule 1). One must show that this holds for suitable \(i\), locally on \(\mathrm{S}'\). It therefore suffices to treat the case where \(\mathrm{S}'\) is the spectrum of a local ring, and then the fact that \(c_i \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}')\) is verified on the closed fiber. One is therefore reduced to the case \(\mathrm{S}' = \operatorname{Spec} k\), \(k\) a field. Now with the notations above, take \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathrm{S}_1\). We have \(\dim \mathrm{C}_1 = \dim \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}_1}\), and U is relatively sch. dense in \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}_1}\). Hence \(\mathrm{U} \cap \mathrm{C}_1 \neq \varnothing\), whence \(\mathrm{U} \cap \{c_p \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}_1\} \neq \varnothing\) and we are done.

The proof of the theorem is now easy. Note first the following consequence of the finiteness of Lemma (3.9): If \(\{\mathrm{A}_i\}\) is an inductive system of rings over S, if \(\underset{\longrightarrow}{\mathrm{A}} = \varinjlim \mathrm{A}_i\), and if the hypotheses of (3.9) are satisfied for \(\mathrm{S} = \operatorname{Spec} \underset{\longrightarrow}{\mathrm{A}}\), then one can descend the object representing the quotient G of \(\mathrm{R} \rightrightarrows \mathrm{X}^2\) to one of the \(\mathrm{S}_i = \operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{A}_i\) with the finiteness and flatness properties stated in (3.9). This is the usual passage to the limit (EGA IV 8 and 11). It follows that for the proof of (iii) and (iv) of (3.7), one may restrict to the case \(\mathrm{S} = \operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{A}\), with A a strictly local ring. So let \(x_0\) be a closed point of the closed fiber \(\mathrm{X}_0\) of X/S. There exists∗ an extension \(\mathrm{A}'\) of A, local, free and finite, and a section of \(\mathrm{X}' = \mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{S}'\) passing through the unique point \(x'_0\) of \(\mathrm{X}'\) above \(x_0\). Note that \(\mathrm{X}'_0 \to \mathrm{X}_0\) is radicial since S is strictly local, so the residue field of A is separably closed. It follows that there exists an inductive system \(\{\mathrm{A}_i\}\) of local rings, flat and finite over S such that, setting \(\underset{\longrightarrow}{\mathrm{A}} = \varinjlim \mathrm{A}_i\) and \(\underset{\longrightarrow}{\mathrm{X}} = \mathrm{X} \times_{\mathrm{S}} \operatorname{Spec} \underset{\longrightarrow}{\mathrm{A}}\), \(\underset{\longrightarrow}{\mathrm{X}}\) has a set of sections which induces a dense set on the closed fiber \(\mathrm{X}_0\). For each closed point \(x_0\) of \(\mathrm{X}_0\) one takes an extension \(\mathrm{A}(x_0)\) such that the corresponding base extension of X admits a section "passing through \(x_0\)", and one takes as inductive system the system of finite tensor products of the \(\mathrm{A}(x_0)\). Note that \(\underset{\longrightarrow}{\mathrm{A}}\) is local, being a limit of local rings. Hence by (3.12) and (3.10) we have the quotient G over \(\operatorname{Spec} \underset{\longrightarrow}{\mathrm{A}}\), hence over one of the \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathrm{A}_i\) by the remarks above, hence locally for the fppf topology.

In fact it results from the constructions that locally for the fppf topology one can find a finite set of sections \(\{c_i\}\) \((i = 1, \ldots, r)\) such that G is covered by the \(c_i \times \mathrm{X}\). (ii) and (iv) follow easily from this fact, and we leave the verification of (i) to the reader.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.13

The sheaf of groups G determined by a group germ \((\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{W})\) over S is representable in each of the following situations:

(i) S is artinian.

(ii) For each local scheme \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) of a closed point \(s\) of S, \(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) has a set of sections which induces on the closed fiber a set whose closure is of dimension \(\dim \mathrm{X}_s\).

(iii) S is strictly local, and X/S is smooth.

(iv) There exists an fppf-covering \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) such that \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is representable and affine over S.

Indeed, (ii) is a consequence of (3.10) and (3.12), (iii) results directly from (ii) and from the "Hensel lemma", (iv) from the descent of affine schemes, and (i) from the descent of group schemes, which is possible here because one knows that every finite subset of a group over a field is contained in an affine open subset (Exp VI).
